metadata.generated_from: Gemini research pack + Claude data engineering prompt

subjects.name: Demography

questions.subject: Demography
questions.question_text: A population pyramid displays a population broken down by which two characteristics?
questions.explanation: A population pyramid is a back-to-back bar chart showing the number of people in each age group, split by sex (males on one side, females on the other), so it reveals the age–sex structure at a glance. Income and education (A) and religion and language (C) are social characteristics not shown on a standard pyramid, and urban–rural residence (D) is a separate geographic breakdown.
questions.hint_seed: Think about the two axes of the back-to-back bar chart used in population studies.

questions.subject: Demography
questions.question_text: The infant mortality rate measures deaths among children in which age range, per 1,000 live births?
questions.explanation: The infant mortality rate counts deaths of infants before their first birthday (under 1 year) per 1,000 live births in a year. Under-5 deaths (B) are captured by the separate under-five mortality rate, and the 1–4 year band (D) describes child mortality after infancy. Under 10 years (C) is not a standard demographic indicator.
questions.hint_seed: Focus on the word 'infant' and the very first year of life.

questions.subject: Demography
questions.question_text: The total fertility rate (TFR) is best described as the average number of children:

options.A: Born in a country in a single year
options.B: A woman would have over her reproductive lifetime at current rates

questions.explanation: TFR estimates the average number of children a woman would bear over her lifetime if she experienced the current age-specific fertility rates, so it is a per-woman lifetime measure. It is not an annual national count (A) or an average household size (C), and survival to age five (D) is a child-mortality concept rather than a fertility measure.
questions.hint_seed: It is a lifetime, per-woman estimate — not a yearly national total.

questions.subject: Demography
questions.question_text: According to the Cambodia Demographic and Health Survey (CDHS) 2021–22, the country's total fertility rate was reported as approximately:
questions.explanation: The CDHS 2021–22 reported a TFR of approximately 2.7 children per woman, reflecting a long decline from much higher historical levels. A value near 4.0 (C) or 5.2 (D) would reflect Cambodia decades earlier, not the 2021–22 survey, while 1.5 (A) is well below replacement and not what the survey reported. The stem names the source and year so the comparison is fair.
questions.local_context_note: Cambodia-specific figure; confirm against the published CDHS 2021–22 report.
questions.hint_seed: Recall the figure tied specifically to the 2021–22 national survey — it sits just above replacement level.

questions.subject: Demography
questions.question_text: According to the CDHS 2021–22, Cambodia's maternal mortality ratio was reported as roughly which value (per 100,000 live births)?
questions.explanation: The CDHS 2021–22 reported a maternal mortality ratio of about 154 per 100,000 live births. Modeled WHO/World Bank estimates for later years differ, which is why the stem fixes a single source and year rather than mixing them. Values around 350–600 (C, D) reflect Cambodia in earlier decades, and 45 (A) is far below the survey figure.
questions.local_context_note: Survey-based MMR; do not mix with WHO/World Bank modeled estimates in the same answer key.
questions.hint_seed: Use only the survey named in the stem; the figure is in the mid-hundreds per 100,000.

questions.subject: Demography
questions.question_text: The age dependency ratio compares the number of dependents to which group?

options.D: The number of births each year

questions.explanation: The age dependency ratio expresses the number of dependents (typically those younger than 15 and older than 64) relative to the working-age population, about 15–64 years. Comparing dependents to the total population (A) or to households (C) would not isolate the support burden on workers, and annual births (D) is unrelated to the ratio's denominator.
questions.hint_seed: Think about who economically supports the young and the elderly.

questions.subject: Demography
questions.question_text: A country is positioned to gain a 'demographic dividend' mainly when:

options.A: The elderly population becomes the largest group
options.B: A large share of the population is of working age with relatively few dependents
options.C: The birth rate suddenly rises sharply

questions.explanation: A demographic dividend is the potential economic boost when a large share of the population is of working age and the dependency burden is comparatively low, provided there is investment in jobs, health, and education. A dominant elderly group (A) raises dependency, a sudden birth-rate rise (C) increases young dependents, and halting migration (D) does not define the dividend.
questions.hint_seed: Consider the balance between workers and the people who depend on them.

questions.subject: Demography
questions.question_text: Over recent decades, the trend in facility-based (institutional) deliveries in Cambodia has generally been one of:

options.D: Complete elimination of all home births

questions.explanation: National survey data describe a steady rise in the proportion of births taking place in health facilities, alongside falling maternal and child mortality. A decrease (B) or no change (C) would contradict the documented improvement, and 'complete elimination of all home births' (D) overstates the trend, since some home births still occur even as facility delivery becomes the norm.
questions.local_context_note: Direction of trend is well supported; exact percentages need instructor verification.
questions.hint_seed: Think about the direction that accompanies falling maternal mortality.

questions.subject: Demography
questions.question_text: A province shows higher child mortality and fewer skilled birth attendants than the capital. Which concept best frames this pattern for health planning?

options.B: Health inequity between rural and urban areas

questions.explanation: Systematic, avoidable differences in outcomes and access between rural provinces and the capital are described as health inequity, linked to where people live and what services they can reach. Herd immunity (A) concerns infectious-disease protection thresholds, the demographic dividend (C) is an age-structure opportunity, and case fatality rate (D) measures deaths among diagnosed cases — none of which frame geographic access gaps.
questions.hint_seed: Focus on the fairness of access tied to where people live.

questions.subject: Demography
questions.question_text: A health district reviews its population pyramid and sees a very wide base (many young children). This should most directly prompt investment in:

options.B: Maternal, newborn, and child health services

questions.explanation: A wide pyramid base signals a large young population and many women of reproductive age, so the most directly indicated investment is in maternal, newborn, and child health services. Geriatric care (A) and pensions (C) match an ageing population with a narrow base, and specialised adult cardiac surgery (D) does not follow directly from a young age structure.
questions.hint_seed: Match the youngest, widest part of the pyramid to the services those ages need.

questions.subject: Fundamental of Nursing Sciences
questions.question_text: According to WHO, which action is the single most effective measure to prevent healthcare-associated infections?
questions.explanation: WHO identifies hand hygiene as the single most effective, evidence-based action to reduce transmission of healthcare-associated infections. Wearing gloves continuously (A) does not replace hand hygiene and can spread organisms if gloves are not changed, routine antibiotics (C) drive resistance rather than prevent spread, and isolating every patient (D) is neither feasible nor the primary measure.
questions.source_reference: WHO Five Moments for Hand Hygiene
questions.hint_seed: Think about the simplest action done before and after every patient contact.

questions.subject: Fundamental of Nursing Sciences
questions.question_text: Which set correctly lists the four classic vital signs?

options.B: Height, weight, body mass index, waist size

questions.explanation: The four classic vital signs are temperature, pulse (heart rate), respiration, and blood pressure. Anthropometric measures such as height and weight (B) describe body size, blood glucose and electrolytes (C) are laboratory values, and pain, mood, sleep, and appetite (D) are useful assessments but are not the classic vital signs.
questions.hint_seed: Recall the four bedside measurements taken on almost every patient.

questions.subject: Fundamental of Nursing Sciences
questions.question_text: Regularly repositioning a bedbound patient is done mainly to prevent:
questions.explanation: Repositioning relieves sustained pressure over bony areas, restoring blood flow to the skin and helping prevent pressure injuries (bedsores). Fever (B) is driven by infection or inflammation, low blood sugar (C) relates to nutrition and medication, and hearing loss (D) is unrelated to positioning, so none of these is the main reason for turning a patient.
questions.hint_seed: Think about what sustained pressure on the skin can cause.

questions.subject: Fundamental of Nursing Sciences
questions.question_text: In WHO's 'My Five Moments for Hand Hygiene', the moment 'before touching a patient' exists primarily to protect:

options.A: The patient from organisms carried on the worker's hands

questions.explanation: Moment 1, before touching a patient, protects the patient from harmful organisms carried on the health worker's hands, so it is patient-directed. Protecting the worker (B) is the aim of moments performed after contact or after touching surroundings, while the laundry (C) and waiting-room visitors (D) are not the focus of this specific moment.
questions.source_reference: WHO Five Moments for Hand Hygiene
questions.hint_seed: Ask who is protected when hands are cleaned just before contact begins.

questions.subject: Fundamental of Nursing Sciences
questions.question_text: Hand hygiene 'before a clean or aseptic procedure' (Moment 2) is performed mainly to:

options.A: Protect the patient from organisms entering their body during the procedure
options.B: Clean the worker's hands only after the procedure
options.C: Replace the need for sterile equipment
options.D: Serve as a way to document the procedure

questions.explanation: Moment 2 is performed immediately before a clean or aseptic task to protect the patient from organisms — including their own — entering a normally sterile site. It is done before, not only after (B); hand hygiene complements but never replaces sterile equipment (C); and it is a safety action, not a documentation step (D).
questions.source_reference: WHO Five Moments for Hand Hygiene
questions.hint_seed: Focus on protecting a sterile site at the very start of a procedure.

questions.subject: Fundamental of Nursing Sciences
questions.question_text: Before giving a medication, the safest first step to confirm you have the right patient is to:

options.A: Ask the patient in the next bed
options.B: Use at least two approved patient identifiers
options.C: Rely on the bed number alone
options.D: Match by the patient's appearance only

questions.explanation: Using at least two approved identifiers (for example, full name plus date of birth or a hospital ID number) is the standard safe practice to confirm identity before any intervention. Asking a neighbouring patient (A) is unreliable, the bed number alone (C) fails if patients move, and matching by appearance (D) is error-prone, so each increases the risk of a wrong-patient error.
questions.source_reference: WHO Five Moments for Hand Hygiene
questions.hint_seed: One identifier can fail — think about how many you should check.

questions.subject: Fundamental of Nursing Sciences
questions.question_text: 'Standard precautions' in infection prevention are best described as measures applied:

options.A: Only to patients with a confirmed infection
options.B: To all patients regardless of known infection status

questions.explanation: Standard precautions are applied to every patient at all times, treating all blood and body fluids as potentially infectious, because infection status is often unknown. Restricting them to confirmed infections (A), to theatres (C), or to outbreaks (D) would leave many real exposures unprotected, which is exactly what the universal 'standard' approach is designed to avoid.
questions.source_reference: WHO Five Moments for Hand Hygiene
questions.hint_seed: The word 'standard' hints at how widely the measures should apply.

questions.subject: Fundamental of Nursing Sciences
questions.question_text: A nurse records a patient's worsening vital signs. The most appropriate next action is to:

options.A: Wait until the end of the shift to mention it
options.B: Document the findings and escalate to the responsible clinician promptly
options.D: Only mention it informally to another nurse

questions.explanation: Accurate documentation paired with prompt escalation to the responsible clinician allows timely review and intervention for a deteriorating patient. Waiting until shift end (A) risks dangerous delay, erasing prior readings (C) destroys the record and the trend, and an informal mention to a colleague (D) does not ensure the decision-maker is alerted.
questions.hint_seed: Recording is only half of it — think about who must be told, and how soon.

questions.subject: Fundamental of Nursing Sciences
questions.question_text: On a ward where alcohol-based handrub is temporarily unavailable, the most appropriate hand-hygiene action is to:

options.A: Skip hand hygiene until supplies return
options.B: Wash hands with soap and clean running water
options.C: Wipe hands on a clean gown
options.D: Keep the same gloves on between patients

questions.explanation: When handrub is unavailable, washing with soap and clean running water is the appropriate alternative, and is in fact required whenever hands are visibly soiled. Skipping hand hygiene (A) directly endangers patients, wiping on a gown (C) does not remove organisms, and reusing gloves between patients (D) actively transfers organisms — all unsafe substitutes for proper handwashing.
questions.source_reference: WHO Five Moments for Hand Hygiene
questions.hint_seed: If one recognized method is missing, recall the other recognized method of cleaning hands.

questions.subject: Fundamental of Nursing Sciences
questions.question_text: Which statement best reflects the development of formal nursing education in Cambodia after 1979?

options.A: It continued without any interruption throughout the 1970s
options.B: It had to be substantially rebuilt because training had been severely disrupted
options.C: It was never formally organized at any point
options.D: It was fully replaced by overseas training only

questions.explanation: After the severe disruption of the 1975–1979 period, formal health-worker training in Cambodia — including nursing — had to be substantially rebuilt from a very weakened base. Saying training continued uninterrupted (A) contradicts the historical record, that it was never organized (C) ignores both earlier and later programs, and that it relied only on overseas training (D) overlooks the domestic rebuilding effort.
questions.local_context_note: Cambodia-specific; confirm exact institutions and dates with instructor.
questions.hint_seed: Consider the effect of the late-1970s disruption on training capacity.

questions.subject: History
questions.question_text: Cambodia's early formal training for medical officers is commonly dated to the founding of the School for Medical Officers in:
questions.explanation: The School for Medical Officers is commonly cited as established in 1946, marking early formal medical training in Cambodia. 1979 (C) marks the end of the Khmer Rouge period rather than a founding, 2002 (D) is far too late for these origins, and 1906 (A) predates the commonly cited date. Exact founding dates should be confirmed with course materials.
questions.source_reference: History of medical education in Cambodia
questions.local_context_note: Date commonly cited; verify with instructor.
questions.hint_seed: Think mid-1940s, around the end of the Second World War.

questions.subject: History
questions.question_text: Which phrase best describes the impact of the 1975–1979 period on Cambodia's trained medical workforce?

options.B: Near-total loss of the professional medical workforce, with fewer than 50 trained doctors often cited as surviving
options.C: Rapid expansion of the medical workforce
options.D: No measurable effect on the number of doctors

questions.explanation: Historical accounts describe a near-total collapse of Cambodia's professional medical workforce during 1975–1979, with fewer than 50 trained doctors often cited as surviving. Describing it as minor (A) or having no effect (D) contradicts the historical record, and 'rapid expansion' (C) is the opposite of what occurred. The stem uses a qualitative range rather than forcing a disputed exact number.
questions.source_reference: History of medical education in Cambodia
questions.hint_seed: Recall the scale of loss — described qualitatively, not as one exact number.

questions.subject: History
questions.question_text: Formal medical training in Cambodia began to be re-established starting around which year?
questions.explanation: Rebuilding of formal medical training is commonly dated to around 1980, soon after the end of the 1975–1979 period, from a severely weakened base. 1953 (A) relates to earlier institutional history, while 1995 (C) and 2010 (D) are too late for the initial reopening. Exact dates should be confirmed against course notes.
questions.source_reference: History of medical education in Cambodia
questions.local_context_note: Date commonly cited; verify with instructor.
questions.hint_seed: Think immediately after the late-1970s disruption ended.

questions.subject: History
questions.question_text: The progression of Cambodian medical institutions is commonly described as moving from a School for Medical Officers toward a Royal School of Medicine in the:
questions.explanation: Following the School for Medical Officers, the Royal School of Medicine is commonly dated to the early 1950s (around 1953), reflecting growing formalization of training. The 1850s (A) predate these institutions, while the 1990s (C) or 2010s (D) come long after this step. Specific years should be verified with course materials.
questions.source_reference: History of medical education in Cambodia
questions.local_context_note: Date commonly cited; verify with instructor.
questions.hint_seed: It follows the 1946 school by roughly a decade.

questions.subject: History
questions.question_text: The establishment of a Faculty of Medicine and Pharmacy in Cambodia is commonly dated to the:
questions.explanation: A Faculty of Medicine and Pharmacy is commonly dated to the early 1960s (around 1962), continuing the formalization of higher medical education before the disruption of the 1970s. The 1920s (A) are far too early, while the late 1980s (C) and 2000s (D) fall after the rebuilding period began. Confirm exact years with course notes.
questions.source_reference: History of medical education in Cambodia
questions.local_context_note: Date commonly cited; verify with instructor.
questions.hint_seed: Think early 1960s, before the 1970s disruption.

questions.subject: History
questions.question_text: In discussions of early Cambodian medical training, 'Sala Krahom' is best understood as:

options.A: A modern teaching hospital opened in 2010
options.B: A building (the 'Red Building') associated with early medical training

questions.explanation: 'Sala Krahom' translates as 'Red Building' and is associated with early medical-training premises in Cambodia, so it is a place reference. It is not a modern hospital opened in 2010 (A), a vaccination campaign (C), or a category of traditional medicine (D). Exact historical details should be verified with course materials.
questions.source_reference: History of medical education in Cambodia
questions.local_context_note: Verify exact building history with instructor.
questions.hint_seed: Translate the Khmer phrase literally — it names a structure by its colour.

questions.subject: History
questions.question_text: After 1979, rebuilding Cambodia's health workforce required strong attention to nursing because:

options.A: Nurses become unnecessary once doctors are trained
options.B: Nursing capacity had also been severely depleted and is essential to delivering care
options.C: Nursing is unrelated to hospital services
options.D: Only overseas nurses were permitted to work

questions.explanation: Rebuilding emphasized nursing because nursing capacity had also been severely depleted, and nurses are essential to delivering everyday clinical care alongside doctors. Claiming nurses are unnecessary (A) or unrelated to hospital services (C) misunderstands their central role, and restricting work to overseas nurses (D) does not reflect the domestic rebuilding effort.
questions.source_reference: History of medical education in Cambodia
questions.hint_seed: Consider whether a health system can function on doctors alone.

questions.subject: History
questions.question_text: The modern Cambodian medical doctor (MD) pathway is commonly described as a long, multi-year program lasting approximately:
questions.explanation: The modern MD pathway in Cambodia is commonly described as roughly an 8-year program, reflecting pre-clinical study, clinical training, and required internship and thesis components. A 2-year (A) or 4-year (B) program is far too short for a full medical degree with clinical training, while 12 years (D) overstates the typical length. The current structure should be verified with the university.
questions.local_context_note: Program length commonly cited; verify current UHS curriculum.
questions.hint_seed: Recall the full length that includes pre-clinical, clinical, and internship stages.

questions.subject: History
questions.question_text: Within the modern Cambodian medical pathway, the Bachelor of Medical Science (BMedSc) is commonly described as a degree awarded:

options.B: After roughly the first six years, with internship and a thesis still required for the full MD
options.D: Instead of ever completing the MD

questions.explanation: The BMedSc is commonly described as a milestone awarded after roughly the first six years of study, with an internship and thesis still required before the full MD is conferred. It is not granted before university begins (A), is not a nursing-only award (C), and does not replace the MD (D) — it is a step within the longer pathway. Confirm specifics with the university.
questions.local_context_note: Pathway structure commonly cited; verify current UHS curriculum.
questions.hint_seed: Think of it as a midpoint milestone, not the final qualification.

questions.subject: History
questions.question_text: Taken together, the milestones of Cambodian medical education (1946 school, 1950s–60s expansion, 1975–79 disruption, post-1980 rebuilding, later UHS formalization) best illustrate:

options.A: Uninterrupted, steady growth with no setbacks
options.B: A pattern of early growth, severe disruption, and determined rebuilding
options.C: A system created entirely after the year 2000

questions.explanation: The sequence illustrates early institutional growth, a severe disruption in 1975–1979, and determined rebuilding that led to today's University of Health Sciences. Describing it as uninterrupted growth (A) ignores the disruption, 'created entirely after 2000' (C) ignores the earlier institutions, and 'purely foreign-run' (D) overlooks the strong domestic rebuilding effort.
questions.source_reference: History of medical education in Cambodia
questions.hint_seed: Look for the overall shape across all the milestones, not one single date.

questions.subject: Infection Disease Agents
questions.question_text: Dengue is caused by the dengue virus and is transmitted to humans mainly by:
questions.explanation: Dengue virus is transmitted mainly by Aedes mosquitoes (notably Aedes aegypti), which bite during the day. Anopheles mosquitoes (A) transmit malaria, not dengue; houseflies (C) are linked to faecal–oral spread of other pathogens; and freshwater snails (D) are intermediate hosts for flukes, not dengue vectors.
questions.hint_seed: Match the virus to the day-biting mosquito genus — not the malaria one.

questions.subject: Infection Disease Agents
questions.question_text: Malaria in humans is caused by which type of organism?
questions.explanation: Malaria is caused by single-celled Plasmodium parasites (protozoa), transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes. It is not a virus (A) like dengue, not a bacterium (B) like the agent of tuberculosis, and not a fungus (D). Correctly classifying the agent is essential to understanding transmission and prevention.
questions.hint_seed: It is a single-celled parasite, not a virus or a bacterium.

questions.subject: Infection Disease Agents
questions.question_text: Tuberculosis is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis and most commonly spreads through:
questions.explanation: Tuberculosis spreads mainly through airborne droplet nuclei released when a person with active pulmonary disease coughs, sneezes, or speaks. Mosquito bites (A) transmit malaria and dengue, undercooked fish (C) transmits liver flukes, and contaminated soil (D) is not the primary route — none of which is how tuberculosis chiefly spreads.
questions.hint_seed: Think about how a cough in a crowded, poorly ventilated room spreads infection.

questions.subject: Infection Disease Agents
questions.question_text: HIV primarily damages the body by attacking which part of the immune system?
questions.explanation: HIV preferentially infects and destroys CD4 (helper) T-lymphocytes, progressively weakening cell-mediated immunity and increasing vulnerability to opportunistic infections. It does not primarily target red blood cells (A) or platelets (C), and bone density (D) is unrelated to the core mechanism of immune damage in HIV.
questions.hint_seed: Focus on the immune cell whose count clinicians track in HIV.

questions.subject: Infection Disease Agents
questions.question_text: Schistosoma mekongi, relevant to the Mekong region, requires which intermediate host to complete its life cycle?
questions.explanation: Schistosoma mekongi uses the freshwater snail Neotricula aperta as its intermediate host, releasing infective larvae into water. A cyprinid fish (B) is the source for the liver fluke Opisthorchis viverrini, not schistosomes; Anopheles mosquitoes (C) transmit malaria; and houseflies (D) are not intermediate hosts for this fluke.
questions.local_context_note: Historically associated with Kratie/Stung Treng along the Mekong; confirm local epidemiology with instructor.
questions.hint_seed: Schistosomes spend part of their life cycle inside a water-dwelling mollusc.

questions.subject: Infection Disease Agents
questions.question_text: Humans most commonly acquire Opisthorchis viverrini (the Southeast Asian liver fluke) by:

options.B: Eating raw or undercooked freshwater fish

questions.explanation: Opisthorchis viverrini is acquired by eating raw or undercooked freshwater cyprinid fish containing infective larvae, which then mature in the bile ducts. It is not mosquito-borne (A) like malaria or dengue, not airborne (C) like tuberculosis, and not acquired from dry soil (D); the key is the dietary, fish-borne route.
questions.local_context_note: Linked to raw-fish dishes; confirm local dietary epidemiology with instructor.
questions.hint_seed: Think about a foodborne route involving freshwater fish.

questions.subject: Infection Disease Agents
questions.question_text: A patient's illness is linked to a habit of eating raw freshwater fish. Which agent is the most fitting consideration?
questions.explanation: Eating raw or undercooked freshwater fish points to Opisthorchis viverrini, the liver fluke acquired by that dietary route. Schistosoma mekongi (A) is acquired through water contact via a snail host rather than by eating fish, Plasmodium (C) is mosquito-borne malaria, and dengue (D) is a mosquito-borne virus, so none fits a fish-borne exposure.
questions.local_context_note: Distinguishing the two flukes by route is a common Mekong-region teaching point.
questions.hint_seed: Match the food history (raw fish) to the correct fluke.

questions.subject: Infection Disease Agents
questions.question_text: Which agent-and-vector pairing is correct?
questions.explanation: Plasmodium parasites are transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes, making C correct. Dengue is carried by Aedes (so A is wrong because it names Anopheles), Plasmodium is not carried by Aedes (so B is wrong), and tuberculosis spreads by airborne droplets rather than snails (so D is wrong). Matching each agent to its true vector prevents common exam errors.
questions.hint_seed: Carefully separate the malaria mosquito from the dengue mosquito.

questions.subject: Infection Disease Agents
questions.question_text: In the Greater Mekong Subregion, the term 'artemisinin partial resistance' refers to reduced responsiveness of which organism to artemisinin-based treatment?
questions.explanation: Artemisinin partial resistance describes delayed clearance of Plasmodium falciparum — the malaria parasite — in response to artemisinin-based therapy, a major concern in the Mekong region. Dengue virus (A) and HIV (D) are viruses not treated with artemisinins, and tuberculosis (C) is a bacterial infection treated with different drugs, so artemisinin resistance does not apply to them.
questions.hint_seed: Artemisinin treats malaria — so the resistant organism is the malaria parasite.

questions.subject: Infection Disease Agents
questions.question_text: Mutations in the PfKelch13 gene (such as C580Y) are used mainly as a molecular marker of:

options.B: Artemisinin partial resistance in Plasmodium falciparum

questions.explanation: PfKelch13 mutations, including C580Y, are validated molecular markers of artemisinin partial resistance in Plasmodium falciparum and are tracked in Mekong-region surveillance. They are specific to the malaria parasite, not a marker of dengue severity (A), tuberculosis drug susceptibility (C), or HIV viral load (D), each of which is monitored by entirely different tests.
questions.hint_seed: The 'Pf' prefix points to which parasite this gene belongs to.

questions.subject: Embryology
questions.question_text: In humans, fertilization most commonly occurs in the:
questions.explanation: Fertilization usually takes place in the ampulla of the uterine (fallopian) tube, where sperm meets the secondary oocyte. The uterus (A) is where the blastocyst later implants, the ovary (B) is where the oocyte is released, and the cervix (D) is the lower opening of the uterus — none is the usual site of sperm–egg fusion.
questions.hint_seed: Think about where egg and sperm first meet soon after ovulation.

questions.subject: Embryology
questions.question_text: The solid ball of cells produced by the early cleavage divisions of the zygote is called the:
questions.explanation: Repeated cleavage divisions of the zygote produce a solid ball of cells called the morula. The blastocyst (B) forms later when a fluid-filled cavity develops, the gastrula (C) refers to the stage of germ-layer formation, and the neural tube (D) is a much later ectodermal structure — so only the morula describes this early solid cluster.
questions.hint_seed: Its Latin name means 'little mulberry' — a solid early cluster of cells.

questions.subject: Embryology
questions.question_text: Which of the following is one of the three primary germ layers?
questions.explanation: The three primary germ layers are ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm, so mesoderm is correct. 'Periderm' (A) is not one of the three layers, the myometrium (C) is the muscular wall of the uterus, and the trophoblast (D) contributes to the placenta rather than being one of the embryonic germ layers.
questions.hint_seed: Recall the trio: ecto-, meso-, endo-.

questions.subject: Embryology
questions.question_text: The blastocyst has an outer cell layer and an inner cell mass. The inner cell mass mainly gives rise to:
questions.explanation: The inner cell mass (embryoblast) gives rise to the embryo proper, while the outer trophoblast contributes to the placenta. So 'placenta only' (A) actually describes the trophoblast, the uterine lining (C) is maternal endometrium, and maternal blood vessels (D) are the mother's tissue — none of which is the fate of the inner cell mass.
questions.hint_seed: Separate the part that becomes the baby from the part that becomes support tissue.

questions.subject: Embryology
questions.question_text: Around the end of the first week, the blastocyst normally begins to implant into the:
questions.explanation: Normal implantation occurs in the endometrium, the inner lining of the uterus, which is prepared to receive the blastocyst. The myometrium (B) is the deeper muscle layer, the cervical canal (C) is the lower outlet, and implantation in the fallopian tube wall (D) would be an abnormal (ectopic) site — so the endometrium is the correct normal location.
questions.hint_seed: Recall the lining layer prepared each cycle to receive an embryo.

questions.subject: Embryology
questions.question_text: Gametogenesis differs from ordinary body-cell division because it uses meiosis to produce cells that are:

options.A: Genetically identical to the parent cell

questions.explanation: Gametogenesis uses meiosis to produce haploid gametes carrying half the chromosome number, so that fertilization restores the normal diploid count. Genetically identical cells (A) result from mitosis, polyploid cells (C) carry abnormal extra sets, and gametes are not without chromosomes (D) — each carries a single set.
questions.hint_seed: Think about why two gametes can combine without doubling the chromosome number each generation.

questions.subject: Embryology
questions.question_text: During neurulation, the neural tube — the future brain and spinal cord — forms from which germ layer?
questions.explanation: The neural tube forms from ectoderm, which also gives rise to the epidermis. Endoderm (A) forms the gut lining and associated organs, mesoderm (B) forms muscle, bone, and the circulatory system, and the trophoblast (D) is placental tissue — so only ectoderm gives rise to the nervous system.
questions.hint_seed: The same layer that forms the skin also forms the nervous system.

questions.subject: Embryology
questions.question_text: By the end of the second week, the embryo is described as a 'bilaminar disc'. This means it consists of:

options.A: A single uniform layer of cells
options.D: A hollow ball with no layers

questions.explanation: At the end of week two the embryonic disc is bilaminar, composed of two layers — epiblast and hypoblast. A single uniform layer (A) precedes this stage, three established germ layers (C) appear later during gastrulation (a trilaminar disc), and a hollow ball with no layers (D) describes the earlier blastocyst — so 'two layers' is the defining feature here.
questions.hint_seed: The prefix 'bi-' tells you how many layers to expect.

questions.subject: Embryology
questions.question_text: Gastrulation is a critical developmental step primarily because it:

options.B: Establishes the three primary germ layers that form all tissues

questions.explanation: Gastrulation transforms the bilaminar disc into a trilaminar disc, establishing ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm — the three germ layers from which all later tissues and organs arise. It is not fertilization (A), which occurs much earlier, nor placental completion (C) or the onset of labour (D), which are unrelated events; its significance is germ-layer formation.
questions.hint_seed: Ask which step first creates the three foundational tissue layers.

questions.subject: Embryology
questions.question_text: Exposure to a teratogen is generally most likely to cause major structural birth defects during which period?

options.B: The embryonic period (roughly weeks 3–8), when organs are forming
options.C: Only in the final week before birth

questions.explanation: The embryonic period (roughly weeks 3–8) is the time of organogenesis, when developing structures are most sensitive to teratogens, so major structural defects are most likely then. Before fertilization (A) there is no embryo to affect, while the final week before birth (C) and after birth (D) fall well past the main organ-forming window and carry lower risk for major structural anomalies.
questions.hint_seed: Match peak vulnerability to the weeks when organs are being built.

sources.title: Cambodia Demographic and Health Survey 2021–22
sources.organization: National Institute of Statistics / Ministry of Health, Cambodia
sources.notes: Primary national source for fertility (TFR ~2.7), maternal mortality (MMR ~154), child mortality, and facility-delivery trends. Exact figures/pages need instructor verification against the published report.

sources.title: WHO / World Bank modeled maternal mortality estimates (2023)
sources.organization: WHO, World Bank, UNICEF, UNFPA, UNDESA (MMEIG)
sources.notes: Modeled MMR estimate (~137 for Cambodia by 2023). Differs from the survey-based CDHS figure — never mix the two in one answer key.

sources.title: WHO Guidelines on Hand Hygiene in Health Care — 'My Five Moments for Hand Hygiene'
sources.notes: Authoritative definition of the Five Moments and hand-hygiene best practice. Stable, widely taught concept.

sources.title: University of Health Sciences (UHS), Phnom Penh — institutional history & curricula
sources.notes: Used for medical-education history and MD/BMedSc pathway framing. Exact dates and current curriculum structure need instructor verification.

sources.title: University of Puthisastra — health sciences programs
sources.notes: Referenced for nursing/health-science education context. Program specifics need instructor verification.

sources.title: History of medical education in Cambodia (institutional milestones)
sources.notes: Covers the School for Medical Officers (1946), Royal School of Medicine (1953), Faculty of Medicine and Pharmacy (1962), the 1975–1979 disruption, and 1980 rebuilding. Dates are commonly cited but should be confirmed against course notes. Survival-number claims are deliberately framed qualitatively ('fewer than 50 doctors often cited').

sources.title: Artemisinin resistance & molecular markers in the Greater Mekong Subregion
sources.notes: Basis for PfKelch13 / C580Y as a validated marker of artemisinin (and associated piperaquine) partial resistance in Plasmodium falciparum. Concept is well established; specific prevalence figures are intentionally avoided.

sources.title: Schistosoma mekongi epidemiology (Mekong basin)
sources.notes: Intermediate host Neotricula aperta snail; historically associated with Kratie/Stung Treng provinces along the Mekong. Used for vector/host distinction items.

sources.title: Opisthorchis viverrini (Southeast Asian liver fluke) life cycle
sources.notes: Transmission via raw/undercooked cyprinid (carp-family) fish; intermediate snail host Bithynia. Used for parasite distinction items.

sources.title: Standard human embryology (foundational developmental biology)
sources.notes: Universally taught concepts (gametogenesis, fertilization, cleavage, blastocyst, implantation, germ layers, neurulation, teratogen timing). Not Cambodia-specific; high confidence.

sources.title: Standard microbiology & infectious-disease agents
sources.notes: Foundational agent/vector facts (dengue virus–Aedes, Plasmodium–Anopheles, Mycobacterium tuberculosis, HIV). High confidence, globally taught.

sources.title: Standard public-health demography concepts
sources.notes: Definitions of population structure, dependency ratio, demographic dividend, TFR, IMR. Concept-level facts are high confidence; country numbers are sourced separately (CDHS/WHO).

sources.title: Svaeng-Yul editorial practice set
sources.notes: Original practice items written for study only, based on the concepts above. Not exam content; requires instructor review before production use.
